para.text: In this chapter, the following terms have the meanings indicated.
Terms Defined.
“1915(c)” means a federal waiver that allows states to provide home- and community-based care to individuals who would otherwise be institutionalized.
“1915(i)” means the 1915(i) Intensive Behavioral Health Services for Children, Youth, and Families program described in this chapter.
“Administrative services organization (ASO)” has the meaning stated in COMAR 10.67.01.01.
“Behavioral Health Administration (BHA)” means the Department’s administration, as described under Health-General Article, Title 7.5, Annotated Code of Maryland.

“Care coordinator” means an individual employed through the care coordination organization that is responsible for providing case management services to 1915(i) participants and families as described in COMAR 10.09.90.
“Caregiver” means an individual with responsibility for 24-hour care and supervision of a minor.

“Care coordination organization (CCO)” means an entity with a minimum of 3 years of experience providing care coordination services that is approved by the Department to provide case management services to 1915(i) participants and their families, pursuant to COMAR 10.09.90.
“Child and family team (CFT)” means a team of individuals selected by the participant and family to work with them to design and implement the plan of care.

“Core service agency (CSA)” means the county or multicounty authority, designated under Health-General Article, Title 10, Subtitle 12, Annotated Code of Maryland, to carry out the duties set forth therein.

“Crisis plan” means a document that is developed by a CFT to address actions that need to be taken in the event that an individual is experiencing a behavioral health crisis, which is included as part of the plan of care.
“Department” means the Maryland Department of Health (MDH) or its designee.
“Department of Human Services (DHS)” has the meaning stated in Human Services Article, Title 2, Annotated Code of Maryland.
“Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services (DPSCS)” has the meaning stated in Correctional Services Article, Title 2, Annotated Code of Maryland.
“Diagnostic Criteria (DC) 0-5” means a system for diagnosing mental health and developmental disorders in infants and toddlers.

“Evidence-based Practice (EBP)" means a set of standardized, replicable interventions for which rigorous scientific research conducted by multiple, independent investigators exists to demonstrate the effectiveness of the interventions, when implemented in accordance with established fidelity standards, in achieving defined, measurable outcomes for the targeted population for which the practice is intended.
“Expressive and experiential behavioral services” means the use of art, dance, music, equine, horticulture, or drama to accomplish individualized goals as part of the plan of care.
“Family” means:
One or more parents and children related by blood, marriage, or adoption, and residing in the same household; or

A parent substitute or substitutes, including informal and formal kinship caregivers as set forth in Health-General Article, §20-105, Annotated Code of Maryland, and Education Article, §7-101, Annotated Code of Maryland, or legal guardians, who have responsibility for the 24 hour care and supervision of a child.
“Family peer support partner” means an individual providing family peer-to-peer support services.

“Family Peer Support Services" means a set of nonclinical activities to support parents and caregivers of youth enrolled in services provided under this chapter in care coordination and the 1915(i) process.
“Family Support Organization (FSO)" means an organization who provides peer support services to parents, caregivers, and youth enrolled in services provided under this chapter.
“Local Behavioral Health Authority (LBHA)” has the meaning stated in Health-General Article, §7.5-101, Annotated Code of Maryland.
“Institution for mental disease (IMD)” has the meaning stated in COMAR 10.67.01.01B.

“Intensive In-Home Services (IIHS)" means a strength-based service intervention provided in the participant's home by a licensed mental health professional and non-licensed in-home stabilizer staff that includes functional assessments, treatment planning, individualized interventions, transition support, and crisis response and intervention.
“Local departments of social services (DSS)” has the meaning stated in Human Services Article, Title 3, Annotated Code of Maryland.
“Maryland Children’s Health Program (MCHP)” has the meaning stated in COMAR 10.09.43.
“Medical Assistance Program” has the meaning stated in COMAR 10.67.02.
“Mental health professional” has the meaning stated in COMAR 10.21.17.02.
“Natural support” means a family member, friend, community member, or organization selected by the participant or family, or both, to participate in the CFT.
“Participant” means an individual who meets the qualifications, as specified in Regulation .03 of this chapter, for benefit eligibility.
“Plan of care (POC)” means a written document that is:
Developed by the CFT that describes the services to be provided to the participant; and
Approved by the Department in accordance with 42 CFR §441.301.
“Program” means the Maryland Medical Assistance Program.
“Provider” means an individual or entity that has enrolled with the Program to provide one or more benefit services covered under this chapter.
“Public Behavioral Health System” has the meaning stated in COMAR 10.09.59.01.
“Residential treatment center” has the meaning stated in Health-General Article, §19-301, Annotated Code of Maryland.
“Room and board” means rent or mortgage, utilities, maintenance, furnishings, and food provided in or associated with an individual’s place of residence.
“Respite care” has the meaning stated in COMAR 10.21.27.
“Serious emotional disturbance (SED)” has the meaning stated in COMAR 10.21.17.
“Service area” means, during the phase-in of the 1915(i), the geographic area in Maryland where the 1915(i) is available.
“State Plan” means the Plan described in §1902(a) of Title XIX of the Social Security Act.

“Supplemental Security Income (SSI)” means a federally administered program providing benefits to needy aged, blind, and disabled individuals under Title XVI of the Social Security Act, 42 U.S.C. §1381 et seq.
“Telehealth” has the meaning stated in COMAR 10.09.49.02.

“Youth peer support” means a set of nonclinical activities provided by individuals in recovery from behavioral health disorders who use their personal lived experience and training to support youth enrolled in services provided under this chapter.
For an applicant to be eligible for 1915(i) services, the applicant shall meet all of the criteria in §§B—H of this regulation.
The applicant shall be younger than 18 years old at the time of enrollment.
The applicant shall reside in a home- and community-based setting that is:
Located in the 1915(i) service area; and
Not any of the following excluded settings:
Therapeutic Group Home (TGH) licensed by the Behavioral Health Administration (BHA) under COMAR 10.21.07;
Psychiatric Respite Care facility located on the grounds of an institution for mental disease (IMD) for the purpose of placement;
Residential program for adults with serious mental illness licensed under COMAR 10.63; or
Group residential facility licensed under COMAR 10.63.
Consent.
For individuals younger than 16 years old, the family or medical guardian of the participant shall give consent for the individual to participate in the 1915(i).
For individuals 16 years old or older, the individual shall give consent to participate in the 1915(i).
The applicant shall:
Have a face-to-face psychosocial assessment completed or updated within 60 days of submission of the enrollment to the ASO that:
Assigns a Diagnostic and Statistical Manual (DSM) behavioral health diagnosis or Diagnostic Criteria (DC) 0-5 diagnosis;
Determines the applicant to be amenable to active clinical treatment; and
Is conducted by a provider not affiliated with the CCO by which the participant may eventually be served; and
Meet the Department’s written medical necessity criteria.
The applicant may not be served in a Health Home as defined in COMAR 10.09.33 while enrolled in the 1915(i).
Medical Assistance Eligibility.

Categorically Needy. An applicant is eligible for 1915(i) services if the applicant is eligible for Medicaid or Maryland Children’s Health Program (MCHP) in accordance with COMAR 10.09.11 or 10.09.24 and has a family income that does not exceed 300 percent of the Federal Poverty Line (FPL).

Optional Categorically Needy. An applicant is eligible for the 1915(i) benefit as optionally categorically needy in accordance with §1902(a)(10)(A)(ii)(XXII) if the individual is receiving services through an existing 1915(c) HCBS waiver program.
The Department may assist applicants in the benefit application process by:
Informing the applicant and family verbally and in writing about services available in the 1915(i);
Assisting the applicant and family to complete the eligibility determination for Medical Assistance for the 1915(i), if necessary; and
Once Medical Assistance eligibility is determined, ensuring that the assessments and documentation required for a medical necessity determination are obtained and provided to the Department.
Based on the criteria established in §§A—H of this regulation:
An applicant’s eligibility for services under this regulation shall be established by the Department;
There is no retroactive eligibility; and
Benefit eligibility may not begin before:
Verification of the applicant’s Medical Assistance eligibility for the 1915(i); and
Completion of the independent evaluation by the Department that the applicant meets all criteria established in §§A—H of this regulation.
If the applicant is determined to meet the needs-based eligibility criteria as established in §§A—H of this regulation, the Department shall:
Obtain written consent from the family or medical guardian to participate in the 1915(i); and
Ensure that the participant is referred immediately upon enrollment determination to a CCO.
The Department shall re-evaluate a participant’s:

Needs-based eligibility for 1915(i) services as specified in §§A—G of this regulation every 12 months, or more frequently due to a significant change in the participant’s condition or needs, in accordance with the Department’s medical necessity criteria; and
Medical Assistance eligibility for 1915(i) services in accordance with the Department’s redetermination policy for all Medical Assistance enrollees.
The 1915(i) shall provide community-based treatment to children with SED or co-occurring diagnosis through the care coordination model.
Enrollment in 1915(i) services qualifies and requires the participant to receive case management services through a CCO, pursuant to COMAR 10.09.90.
Each participant shall have an individualized POC that is managed by the CCO, pursuant to COMAR 10.09.90.
In partnership with the CFT, the CCO shall:
Reevaluate the POC at least every 60 days with re-administration of BHA-approved assessments as appropriate, and more frequently in response to a crisis;
Determine the family vision, which will guide the planning process;
Identify strengths of the entire team;
Determine the needs that the team will work on;
Determine outcome statements for meeting identified needs;
Determine the specific services and supports required in order to achieve the goals identified in the POC;
Create a mission statement that the team generates and commits to following;
Identify the individuals responsible for each of the strategies in the POC;
Review and update the crisis plan; and
Meet at least every 60 days or more frequently as clinically indicated to:
Coordinate the implementation of the POC; and
Re-evaluate and update the POC as necessary.

Benefit participants shall have access to specialty behavioral health services through the Department’s public behavioral health system. Participants shall also be enrolled in the Medical Assistance Program’s managed care program, known as HealthChoice, in accordance with eligibility requirements set forth in COMAR 10.67.02.

section.heading: Denial, Emergency Suspension of Approval, and Disciplinary Action.

para.text: Denial of Approval.
If the Department proposes to deny approval to an applicant under the provisions of this chapter, the Department shall give written notice of the proposed denial to the:
Provider applicant;
Care coordination organization (CCO); and
Administrative service organization (ASO).
In the notice under §A(1) of this regulation, the Department shall include:
The date on which the Department proposes to deny approval;
The facts that warrant the proposed denial of approval;
Citation of the regulation or regulations upon which the proposed denial is based;
Notification that before the denial of approval, the provider may request a hearing under the provisions of COMAR 10.21.16; and
When feasible, notification of a case resolution conference.
Disciplinary Action.
The Department may propose to take any of the following disciplinary actions against a provider:
Revocation of approval;
Suspension of approval;
Probation with conditions; or
Banning new admissions.
The Department may propose to take one of the actions outlined in §B(1) of this regulation if the provider:
Is out of compliance with the requirements of this chapter;
Fails to maintain financial viability; or
Obtains or attempts to obtain approval or payment by fraud, misrepresentation, or the submission of false information to the Department.

Except under §C of this regulation, the Department shall send written notice of the proposed action not less than 45 calendar days in advance of the proposed action taken under this regulation to the:
Provider;
CCO;
ASO; and
Applicable CSA or LBHA.
In the notice under §B(3) of this regulation, the Department shall include:
The date on which the Department proposes to take action and, when feasible, the date of a case resolution conference;
The facts that warrant the proposed action;
Citation of the regulation or regulations upon which the proposed action is based; and
Notification that, before the action, the provider has the right to request a hearing under the provisions of COMAR 10.21.16.
If, after notice and opportunity to be heard, the Department takes disciplinary action, the provider shall, within 10 working days:
Notify individuals or the guardians of individuals receiving services of the action; and
If the program ceases operations:
Notify individuals or the guardians of individuals receiving services of the suspension; and
Cooperate with the CCO, child and family team, and the Department in accessing appropriate alternate services for individuals served by the provider.
Emergency Suspension of Approval.

Under State Government Article, §10-226, Annotated Code of Maryland, upon findings of conditions that pose an imminent risk to the health, safety, or welfare of an individual served by a provider, the Department may order the immediate suspension of the approval of the provider and the cessation of operation.
If the Department takes the action under §C(1) of this regulation, the Department shall promptly give written notice of the proposed emergency suspension to the:
Provider;
CCO;
ASO; and,
Applicable CSA or LBHA.
In the notice under §C(2) of this regulation, the Department shall include:
The proposed effective date of the emergency suspension;
When feasible, the date of a pre-deprivation hearing and a case resolution conference before the Department’s final action;
The findings under §C(1) of this regulation and the reasons that support the findings; and
Notification that:
Following the emergency suspension, the provider may request a hearing under the provisions of COMAR 10.21.16; and
The emergency suspension may lead to revocation of the approval if the violation or violations are not corrected within the time period specified by the Department.
If the Department suspends approval, the provider shall immediately:
Notify individuals or the guardians of individuals receiving services of the suspension;
Cooperate with the CCO, child and family team, and the Department in accessing appropriate alternate services for individuals in the program; and
Cease operations of the program.

section.heading: Covered Services — Family Peer Support Services.
section.text: Family peer support services:

para.text: May be provided without the presence of the participant;
Are delivered by a family peer support partner employed by a family support organization (FSO);
May include, but are not limited to:
Explaining the role and function of the FSO to newly enrolled families and creating linkages to other peers and supports in the community;
Working with the participant and family to identify and articulate concerns, needs, and vision for the future of the participant;
Ensuring family and participant opinions and perspectives are incorporated into the CFT process and POC:
Through communication with the care coordinator and team members; and
By attending CFT meetings with the family to support family decision-making and choice of options;
Listening to the family express needs and concerns and offering suggestions for engagement in the treatment process;
Helping the family identify and engage its own natural support system and facilitating the family attending peer support groups and other FSO activities throughout the POC process;
Working with the family:
To organize and prepare for meetings in order to maximize participation in meetings;
By informing the family about options and possible outcomes in selecting services and supports to assist with informed decision-making; and
By supporting the family in meetings at school and other locations in the community and during court hearings;
Empowering the family to make choices to achieve desired outcomes for the participant as well as the entire family; and

Helping the family to acquire the skills and knowledge needed to attain greater self-sufficiency and maximum autonomy and assisting the family to develop the skills and confidence to independently identify, seek out and access resources that will assist in:
Managing and mitigating the participant's behavioral health condition or conditions;
Preventing the development of secondary or other chronic conditions;
Promoting optimal physical and behavioral health; and
Addressing and encouraging activities related to health and wellness;
Are provided by a family support organization (FSO) that:

Is designated as a private, nonprofit entity designated under §501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Service Code, and submits copies of the certificate of incorporation and Internal Revenue Service designation;
Establishes hiring practices that give preference to:
Current or previous caregivers of a child or youth with behavioral health challenges;
Individuals who have experience with State or local services and systems as a consumer who has or had behavioral health challenges; or
Both §D(2)(a) and (b) of this regulation;
Submits a copy of the organization's personnel policy that sets forth the preferred employment criteria stated in §D(2) of this regulation;
Employs a staff that is comprised of at least 50 percent of individuals who are:
Current or previous caregivers of a child or youth with behavioral health challenges; or
Individuals who have experience with State or local services and systems as a consumer who has or had behavioral health challenges;
Submits a list of staff and positions held with identification of those who fit the experienced caregiver and consumer criteria stated in §D(4) of this regulation; and
Submits a certificate of eligibility that includes:
Attestation of compliance with §D(1)—(4) of this regulation; and

The organization’s mission statement that establishes the purpose of the organization as providing support and education to youth with emotional, behavioral, or mental health challenges and their caregivers; and
Shall be provided by family peer support partners who:
Are employed by an FSO;
Are 18 years old or older;
Receive supervision from an individual who:
Is 21 years old or older; and
Has at least 3 years of experience providing family peer-to-peer support or working with children with serious behavioral health challenges and their families;

Have current or prior experience as a caregiver of a child with behavioral health challenges or be an individual who has experience with State or local services and systems as a consumer who has or had behavioral health challenges; and
Receive training and certification as approved by the Department.

section.heading: Covered Services — Youth Peer Support Services.

para.text: Youth Peer Specialists.
Youth peer support services shall be delivered directly to the participant by a youth peer specialist who is:
Employed by a FSO as defined in COMAR 10.09.89.09D;
A peer recovery specialist who meets one of the criteria in §A(2) of this regulation;
18 years old or older;
Self-identified as a person in long-term recovery from the effects of a behavioral health disorder for a period of 2 years or more; and
An individual who has experience with State or local services and systems as a consumer of behavioral health services.
A youth peer support specialist shall be either:
Certified as a peer support specialist by the BHA or its designee;
In the process of obtaining a peer support specialist certification by the BHA or its designee; or
Under the supervision of a certified youth peer supervisor described in §B of this regulation.
A youth peer specialist may not provide youth peer support services to 1915(i) enrollees older than themselves.
Youth Peer Supervisors. Youth peer support services shall be delivered under the supervision of the youth peer supervisor who shall:
Be 21 years old or older; and
Have at least 3 years of experience providing family or youth peer support or working with children with serious behavioral health challenges and their families;
Youth peer support services may include, but are not limited to:
Promoting wellness through modeling;
Assisting the youth with understanding the person-centered planning process;
Coaching, supporting, and training in order to ensure the youth’s success in navigating various social contexts, learning new skills, and making functional progress;
Coaching the youth to understand the care planning process and articulate goals during the person-centered planning process;
Providing mutual support, hope, reassurance, and advocacy that includes sharing one’s own personal story;
Serving as an advocate, mentor, or facilitator for resolution of issues;
Helping the youth develop self-advocacy skills and gain the ability to play a proactive role in their own treatment; and
Skills development for coping with and managing behavioral health symptoms and trauma, wellness, resiliency, and recovery support.

section.heading: Covered Services — Respite Services.
section.text: Respite services:

para.text: Include a set of specific short-term services documented in the POC that include:
A schedule of the participant's activities during respite;
Medication monitoring, if needed;
The frequency, duration, and intensity of staff support;
Respite locations; and
The aftercare plan or recommendations;
Include community-based respite services, which are provided in the participant's home or other community-based setting;
Include out-of-home respite services, which provide a temporary overnight living arrangement outside of the participant’s home; and
Are provided by organizations that shall:
Meet the requirements of COMAR 10.63.03.15;
Employ respite care specialists who are:
21 years old or older and have a high school diploma or high school equivalency; or

At least 18 years old and enrolled in, or in possession of at least an associate degree from, an accredited college or university in a human services field, and are limited to providing services to participants under 13 years of age;
Ensure that community-based respite services are provided in the participant's home or other community-based setting; and
Ensure that out-of-home respite services are:
Provided in a community-based temporary overnight living arrangement outside the participant's home; and
Where applicable, delivered in accordance with COMAR 14.31.05—14.31.07.

section.heading: Covered Services — Expressive and Experiential Behavioral Services.

para.text: Expressive and experiential behavioral services:
May be provided to an individual or group;
Provide sensory modalities to participants to assist in achieving POC objectives; and
Include:
Art behavioral services;
Dance behavioral services;
Equine-assisted behavioral services;
Horticultural behavioral services;
Music behavioral services; or
Drama behavioral services.
Qualification to Provide Expressive and Experiential Behavioral Services.
To provide expressive and experiential behavioral services, an individual shall have:
A bachelor's or master's degree from an accredited college or university; and
Current registration in the applicable association as outlined in §B(3) of this regulation.
A licensed clinical professional art therapist may provide art behavioral services without meeting the association registration requirements described in §B(3)of this regulation.
Association Registration.
Art Behavioral Services. To provide art behavioral services, an individual shall be currently registered as a registered art therapist by:
The Art Therapy Credentials Board in the American Art Therapy Association; or
A comparable association with equivalent requirements.
For Dance Behavioral Services. To provide dance behavioral services, an individual shall be currently registered as a dance therapist registered, or an academy of dance therapists registered in:
The American Dance Therapy Association; or
A comparable association with equivalent requirements.
For Equine-Assisted Behavioral Services. To provide equine-assisted behavioral services, an individual shall be currently certified by:
The Equine Assisted Growth and Learning Association to provide services under the Equine Assisted Growth and Learning Association model;
Professional Association of Therapeutic Horsemanship International; or

A comparable association with certification requirements at least equivalent to the Equine Assisted Growth and Learning Association or Professional Association of Therapeutic Horsemanship International.
For Horticultural Behavioral Services. To provide horticultural behavioral services, an individual shall be currently registered as a horticultural therapist registered in:
The American Horticultural Therapy Association; or
A comparable association with equivalent requirements.
For Music Behavioral Services. To provide music behavioral services, an individual shall be currently registered as a music therapist-board certified by:
The Certification Board for Music Therapists, Inc; or
A comparable association with equivalent requirements.
For Drama Behavioral Services. To provide psychodrama/drama behavioral services, an individual shall be currently registered as a registered drama therapist or a board certified trainer in:
The National Association for Drama Therapy; or
A comparable association with equivalent requirements.
Intensive In-Home Services (IIHS):
Are strengths-based interventions with the child and his or her identified family that includes a series of components, such as:
Functional assessments and treatment planning;
Individualized interventions;
Crisis response and intervention; or
Transition support;
May be provided to the child alone, to other family members, or to the child and family members together;

Are intended to support a child to remain in his or her home and reduce hospitalizations and out-of-home placements or changes of living arrangements through focused intervention in the home and community;

May be used in situations such as the start of a child's enrollment in the 1915(i), upon discharge from a hospital or residential treatment center, or to prevent or stabilize after a crisis situation; and
Shall include a minimum of two face-to-face contacts with the participant per week of service with at least one of the contacts provided in-person.
Types of IIHS Providers. The Department may approve two types of IIHS providers:
Evidence-Based Practice (EBP)-IIHS providers, to include providers of EBPs as determined by the Department; and
Promising Practice IIHS providers (non-EBP), to include providers of the In-Home Intervention Program for Children and other promising practices, as determined by the Department.
An EBP-IIHS provider shall:

Have a certificate or letter from the national or intermediate surveyor or developer of the particular evidence-based practice to demonstrate that the EBP-IIHS provider meets all requirements for Department-approved EBP-IIHS, to include participating in all fidelity monitoring activities; and
Meet the requirements of §E of this regulation.
A Non-EBP IIHS provider shall:
Be a Department-approved In-Home Intervention Program for Children provider or have a certificate or letter from a national or intermediate purveyor or developer of another promising practice; and
Meet the requirements of §E of this regulation.
All IIHS providers shall:
Ensure that there are Clinical Supervisors and staff who are responsible for creating, implementing, and managing the treatment plan with the child and family and the CFT;
Provide crisis response services for the participants on the IIHS provider's caseload and ensure that on-call and crisis intervention services are:
Provided by a licensed mental health professional trained in the intervention;
Available 24-hours per day, 7 days per week, during the hours the provider is not open to the individual enrolled in the treatment; and
In compliance with staffing, supervision, training, data collection, and fidelity monitoring requirements set forth by the purveyor, developer, or MDH and approved by the Department;
Employ Clinical Supervisors who:
Have a current license under the Health Occupations Article, Annotated Code of Maryland, as a:
Licensed certified social worker-clinical (LCSW-C);
Licensed clinical professional counselor (LCPC);
Psychologist;
Psychiatrist;
Nurse psychotherapist; or
Advanced practice registered nurse/psychiatric mental health (APRN-PMH); and
Have at least 3 years of experience in providing mental health treatment to children and families;
Employ mental health professionals who:
Have a current license under the Health Occupations Article, Annotated Code of Maryland, as a:
Licensed certified social worker (LCSW);
LCSW-C;
LCPC;
Psychologist;
Psychiatrist;
Nurse psychotherapist; or
APRN-PMH.
Are supervised by a clinical lead supervisor;
See the child in-person at least once per 7 days while receiving IIHS services;
Employ in-home stabilizers who:
Support the implementation of the treatment plan, but are not responsible for creating it or modifying it;
Are at least 21 years old;
Have at least a high school diploma or equivalency; and
Have completed relevant, comprehensive, appropriate training before providing services, as outlined by the purveyor, developer, or the Department and approved by the Department; and
Provide services consistent with the State-approved EBP or State-approved promising practice model.
Reimbursement shall be made by the Program only when all of the requirements of this chapter are met.
The Program may not reimburse for:
Services that are:
Provided by a member of the recipient’s immediate family or an individual who resides in the recipient’s home;
Not preauthorized by the Department;
Not medically necessary;
Beyond the provider’s scope of practice;
Not appropriately documented;
Part of another service paid for by the State; or
Provided without a valid required license or appropriate credentials as specified in this chapter;
Completion of forms or reports;
Broken or missed appointments;
Time spent in travel by the provider to and from site of service, except when with the participant or the participant’s family; or
Costs of travel by the provider to and from the site of service.
The Program may not reimburse more than the following:
1 session per day for out-of-home respite;
6 hours per day of community-based respite;
24 overnight units of respite annually;
2 types per day of expressive and experiential behavioral services; or
1 MCRS assessment for the development of the initial crisis plan.
Intensive in-home services may not be reimbursed for the same day of service or on the same day of service as:
Partial hospitalization/day treatment;
Mobile treatment services; or
Other family therapies.
The Program may not reimburse for IIHS:
If fewer than two face-to-face contacts occur per week; and
None of the weekly face-to-face contacts are in-person.
Out-of-home respite and community-based respite services may not be reimbursed for the same day of service or on the same day of service as:
Residential rehabilitation;
Therapeutic behavioral services; or
Any other public behavioral health system respite services.
Out-of-home respite and community-based respite services do not include on-going day care or before or after school programs.
Out-of-home respite and community-based respite services may not be delivered to youth residing in Treatment Foster Care.
The Program may not reimburse for the following services via telehealth:
Out-of-home respite;
Community-based respite services; and
Equine-assisted behavioral services.
The Program may not reimburse for the following services via audio-only telehealth:
Art behavioral services;
Dance behavioral services;
Horticulture behavioral services;
Music behavioral services; and
Drama behavioral services.
Request for Payment.
An approved provider shall submit requests for payment for the services covered under this chapter according to the procedures set forth in COMAR 10.09.36.04.
The provider shall:
Bill the ASO in accordance with the payment methodology specified in this chapter;
Accept payment from the ASO as payment in full for the covered services rendered, and make no additional charge to the participant or any other party for these services; and
Submit a request for payment in a manner approved by the Program, which includes a certification of the:
Date or dates of service;
Participant's name and Medicaid number;
Provider's name, location, and provider identification number;
Type, procedure code or codes, and unit or units of covered services provided; and
Amount of reimbursement requested.
Documentation Required.
Payments by the Program or its designee may be withheld or recovered if the provider fails to submit:
Requested evidence of services provided;
Staff qualifications;
Corrective action plans; or
Any other types of documentation related to ensuring the health and safety of a participant.
Payments shall be released upon receipt and approval by the Program or its designee of the requested documentation.
An appeal by the provider under COMAR 10.01.03 does not stay the withholding of payments.
Billing time limitations for the services covered under this chapter are the same as those set forth in COMAR 10.09.36.06.
Payments.
Payments shall be made only for services rendered by a 1915(i) provider approved by the Department and enrolled as a Medicaid provider.
Services will only be paid when delivered in accordance with the POC that has been authorized by the Department.
The Program shall pay according to the fee-for-service schedule for each of the covered services, as set forth in this regulation.
Family peer support services as described in Regulation .09 of this chapter shall be reimbursed at the following rates:
For dates of service from July 1, 2023 through December 31, 2023:
$22.30 per 15-minute unit for face-to-face services; or
$11.14 per 15-minute unit for telephonic or other non-face-to-face activities.
For dates of service from January 1, 2024 through June 30, 2024:
$24.08 per 15-minute unit for face-to-face services; or
$11.14 per 15-minute unit for telephonic or other non-face-to-face activities.
Effective July 1, 2024:
$24.80 per 15-minute unit of family peer support or face-to-face services; or
$12.39 per 15-minute unit for telephonic or other non-face-to-face activities.
Youth peer support services as described in Regulation .10 of this chapter shall be reimbursed at $24.80 per 15-minute unit of youth peer support services.
Respite services as described in Regulation .11 of this chapter shall be reimbursed at the following rates:
For dates of service from July 1, 2023 through December 31, 2023:
$35.14 per 1-hour unit of service for community-based respite services; or
$287.57 per unit of out-of-home respite care.
For dates of service from January 1, 2024 through June 30, 2024:
$37.95 per 1-hour unit of service for community-based respite services; or
$300.86 per unit of out-of-home respite care.
Effective July 1, 2024:
$39.09 per 1-hour unit of service for community-based respite services; or
$309.89 per unit of out-of-home respite care.
Expressive and experiential behavioral services as described in Regulation .12 of this chapter, when provided by a licensed mental health professional, shall be reimbursed at the following rates:
For dates of service from July 1, 2023 through December 31, 2023:
For individual therapy:
$95.55 per 45—50-minute session; or
$125.20 per 75—80-minute session; and
For group therapy:
$37.98 per 45—60-minute session; or
$49.39 per prolonged (75—90-minute) session.
For dates of service from January 1, 2024 through June 30, 2024:
For individual therapy:
$103.19 per 45—50-minute session; or
$135.22 per 75—80-minute session; and
For group therapy:
$41.02 per 45—60-minute session; or
$53.34 per prolonged (75—90-minute) session.
Effective July 1, 2024:
For individual therapy:
$106.29 per 45—50-minute session; or
$139.28 per 75—80-minute session; and
For group therapy:
$42.25 per 45—60-minute session; or
$54.94 per prolonged (75—90-minute) session.
Expressive and experiential behavioral services as described in Regulation .12 of this chapter, when provided by a non-licensed mental health professional, shall be reimbursed at the following rates:
For dates of service from July 1, 2023 through December 31, 2023:
For individual therapy:
$86.86 per 45-minute session; or
$112.93 per 75—80-minute session; and
For group therapy:
$33.76 per 45—60-minute session; or
$43.87 per prolonged (75—90-minute) session.
For dates of service from January 1, 2024 through June 30, 2024:
For individual therapy:
$93.81 per 45-minute session; or
$121.96 per 75—80-minute session; and
For group therapy:
$36.46 per 45—60-minute session; or
$47.38 per prolonged (75—90-minute) session.
Effective July 1, 2024:
For individual therapy:
$96.62 per 45-minute session; or
$125.62 per 75—80-minute session; and
For group therapy:
$37.55 per 45—60-minute session; or
$48.80 per prolonged (75—90-minute) session.
Intensive in-home services as described in Regulation .13 of this chapter shall be reimbursed at the following rates:
For dates of service from July 1, 2023 through December 31, 2023:
$347.67 per week of service for EBP-IIHS providers; or
$297.87 per week of service for non-EBP IIHS providers.
For dates of service from January 1, 2024 through June 30, 2024:
$375.48 per week of service for EBP-IIHS providers; or
$297.87 per week of service for non-EBP IIHS providers.
Effective July 1, 2024:
$386.74 per week of service for EBP-IIHS providers; or
$306.81 per week of service for non-EBP IIHS providers.

section.heading: Recovery and Reimbursements.
section.text: Recovery and reimbursement are set forth in COMAR 10.09.36.07.

section.heading: Cause for Suspension or Removal and Imposition of Sanctions.
section.text: Cause for suspension or removal and imposition of sanctions is as set forth in COMAR 10.09.36.08 and 10.21.10.

section.heading: Appeal Procedures for Providers.
section.text: Appeal procedures for providers are those set forth in COMAR 10.09.36.09.

section.heading: Appeal Procedures for Applicants and Participants.
section.text: Appeal procedures for applicants and participants are those set forth in COMAR 10.01.04, 10.09.24.13, and 10.09.70.

para.text: In this chapter, the following terms have the meanings indicated.
Terms Defined.
“1915(i)” means the 1915(i) Intensive Behavioral Health Services for Children, Youth, and Families program defined in COMAR 10.09.89.

“Care coordination” means services which assist participants in gaining access to a full range of behavioral health services and, as necessary, any medical, social, financial assistance, counseling, educational, housing, and other support services.
“Care coordination organization (CCO)” means an entity with a minimum of 3 years of experience providing care coordination services that:
Are approved by the Department under this chapter; and
Meet the requirements of COMAR 10.09.89 to provide care coordination to participants in the 1915(i) Intensive Behavioral Health Services for Children, Youth, and Families program.

“Care coordinator” means an individual employed through the care coordination organization who is responsible for providing care management services to benefit participants and families, including, but not limited to:
Coordination of child and family team meetings; and
Completion of the initial and revised plan of care.

“Child and Adolescent Service Intensity Instrument (CASII)” means a standardized assessment tool that provides a determination of the appropriate level of service intensity needed by a child or adolescent and their family.

“Child and family team (CFT)” means the group of individuals, including both formal and informal supports and inclusive of the youth and the youth’s family, responsible for the creation and implementation of the plan of care.

“Co-occurring disorder” means a diagnosis based on the current Diagnostic and Statistical Manual published by the American Psychiatric Association where the participant is indicated as having both a mental illness and substance use disorder.

“Core service agency (CSA)” means the county or multicounty authority, designated under Health-General Article, Title 10, Subtitle 12, Annotated Code of Maryland, to carry out the duties set forth therein.
“Department” has the meaning stated in COMAR 10.09.36.01 and refers to the Department or its designee.
“Early Childhood Service Intensity Instrument (ECSII)” means a standardized assessment tool used to determine the intensity of services needed for infants, toddlers, and children from ages 0—5 years.
“Local Behavioral Health Authority (LBHA)” has the meaning stated in Health-General Article, §7.5-101, Annotated Code of Maryland.
“Medical Assistance Program” has the meaning stated in COMAR 10.09.36.01.
“Mental Health Case Management: Care Coordination for Children and Youth” has the same meaning as “care coordination” as defined in this regulation.
“Mental health professional” has the meaning stated in COMAR 10.21.17.02.
“Mental health services” means those services described in COMAR 10.09.70.
“Minor” means a child or adolescent younger than 16 years old.
“Natural supports” means any individual who plays a positive, but nonprofessional, role in someone’s plan of care.
“Participant” means an individual who meets the qualifications for participation in care coordination that are specified in Regulation .03 of this chapter.

“Plan of care (POC)” means the individualized plan for supports and services prepared according to the requirements outlined in this chapter for a specific participant in care coordination, including a POC developed for 1915(i) participants pursuant to COMAR 10.09.89.
“Program” has the meaning stated in COMAR 10.09.36.01.
“Provider” means the care coordination provider.
“Recipient” has the meaning stated in COMAR 10.09.36.01.
“Residential treatment center (RTC)” has the meaning stated in COMAR 10.07.04.
“Serious emotional disturbance (SED)” has the meaning stated in COMAR 10.21.17.02.
“Young adult” means an individual who is 18 years old or older but not older than 21 years old.

section.heading: Participant Eligibility — Level III — Intensive Care Coordination.

para.text: The participant shall meet at least one of the following conditions:
The participant has been enrolled in the 1915(i) program for 6 months or less;

The participant is currently enrolled in Level I or Level II Care Coordination services under this chapter and has experienced one of the following adverse childhood experiences during the preceding 6 months:
Emotional, physical, or sexual abuse;
Serious emotional or physical neglect; or
Significant family disruption or stressors;
The participant meets the following conditions:
The participant has a behavioral health disorder amenable to active clinical treatment, diagnosed through a face-to-face psychosocial assessment by a licensed mental health professional;
There is clinical evidence the minor has a SED and continues to meet the service intensity needs and medical necessity criteria for the duration of their enrollment;

A comprehensive psychosocial assessment performed by a licensed mental health professional who is not affiliated with the CCO finds that the participant exhibits a significant impairment in functioning, representing potential serious harm to self or others, across settings, including the home, school, or community;

The psychosocial assessment supports the completion of the Early Childhood Service Intensity Instrument (ECSII) for youth ages 0—5 or the Child and Adolescent Service Intensity Instrument (CASII) for youth ages 6—21, by which the participant receives a score of:
2 or higher on the ECSII; or
2 or higher on the CASII; and
Meet the conditions of either §B or §C of this regulation, as applicable.
Youth with a score of 2, 3, or 4 on the CASII shall meet one of the following criteria to be eligible based on their impaired functioning and service intensity level:
Transitioning from a residential treatment center; or
Living in the community, be 6 through 21 years old, and have:
Any combinations of 2 or more inpatient psychiatric hospitalizations , emergency room visits, crisis stabilization center visits, or mobile crisis team responses in the past 12 months; or
Been in an RTC within the past 90 calendar days.
Youth who are younger than 6 years old who have a score of a 2, 3, or 4 on the ECSII shall either:
Be referred directly from one of the following:
Inpatient or day hospital unit;
Primary care physician (PCP);
Outpatient psychiatric facility;
Early Childhood Mental Health (ECMH) Consultation program in daycare;
Head Start program;
Judy Hoyer Center; or
Home visiting program; or
If living in the community, meet one or more of the following criteria in the past 12 months:
Had one or more psychiatric inpatient or day hospitalizations;
Had one or more ER visits;
Had one or more crisis stabilization center visits;
Had one or more mobile crisis team responses;
Exhibit severe aggression;
Display dangerous behavior;
Been suspended from school or childcare setting;
Display emotional or behavioral disturbance prohibiting their care by anyone other than their primary caregiver;
Be at risk for out-of-home placement or placement disruption;
Have severe temper tantrums that place the child or family members at risk of harm;
Have trauma exposures and other adverse life events; or
Be at risk of family-related risk factors, including safety, parent-child relational conflict, and poor health and developmental outcomes.
Selection of CCOs.
The local core service agencies shall select child and youth CCOs through a competitive procurement process, at least once every 5 years.

Regional CCOs may be procured at the mutual agreement of local core service agencies so long as the local core service agencies demonstrate that there is sufficient provider capacity to serve the children and youth in a particular region.
The CCO shall:
Be approved by the Department as a CCO;
Commit to coordination with all agencies involved in the participant’s POC; and
Work with the State and local child- and family-serving agencies to develop a network of clinical and natural supports in the community to address strengths and needs identified in each POC.
Required Criminal Background Checks. The provider shall comply with the criminal background check policy outlined in COMAR 10.63.01.05C.

Prohibitions Against Utilization of Staff. Unless waived by the Department in accordance with §E of this regulation, the provider shall prohibit from working with the participant or the participant’s family any staff, volunteers, students, or individuals who:

Are convicted of, received probation before judgment for, or entered a plea of nolo contendere to a felony or a crime of moral turpitude or theft or have any other criminal history that indicates behavior which is potentially harmful to a participant;
Are cited on any professional licensing or certification boards or any other registries with a determination of abuse, misappropriation of property, financial exploitation, or neglect; or
Have an indicated finding of child abuse or neglect.

Waiver of Employment Prohibitions. The Department may waive the prohibition against working with the participant or the participant’s family if the provider submits a request to the Department together with the following documentation that:
For criminal background checks:

The conviction of, the probation before judgment for, or plea of nolo contendere to the felony or the crime involving moral turpitude or theft was entered more than 10 years before the date of the employment application;
The criminal history does not indicate behavior that is potentially harmful to participants; and
Includes a statement from the individual as to the reasons the prohibition should be waived; and
For abuse and neglect findings:
The indicated finding occurred more than 7 years before the date of the clearance request;
The summary of the indicated finding does not indicate behavior that is potentially harmful to the participant or the participant’s family; and
Includes a statement from the individual as to the reasons the prohibition should be waived.
The CCO shall provide all three levels of care coordination to ensure continuity of care for participants.
The Department shall reimburse for the care coordination services in this regulation when these services have been documented, pursuant to the requirements of this chapter, as necessary.
Care coordination services shall be coordinated with, and may not duplicate activities provided as part of, institutional services and discharge planning activities.
Care coordination may include contacts that are directly related to identifying the needs and supports for helping the participant to access services.
The CCO shall engage in participant advocacy, including:
Empowering the participant and, if the participant is a minor, the minor’s parent or guardian to secure needed services;
Taking any necessary actions to secure services on the participant’s behalf; and

Encouraging and facilitating the participant’s decision making and choices leading to accomplishment of the participant’s goals or, if the participant is a minor, encouraging the parent or guardian to carry out these decisions.
Comprehensive Participant Assessment and Periodic Participant Reassessment.
Providers shall use a child and youth assessment tool approved by the Department to perform participant assessments and reassessments.

Initial assessment or reassessment involves the participant’s stated needs and review of information concerning the participant’s mental health, social, familial, educational, cultural, medical, developmental, legal, vocational, and economic status to assist in the formulation of a POC.

The initial assessment or reassessment of the participant’s needs and progress shall be facilitated by the care coordinator and monitored by the CFT, which includes the participant, family members, and friends of the participant, as appropriate, or, if the participant is a minor, the minor’s parent or guardian, and community service providers, such as mental health providers, medical providers, social workers, and educators, as appropriate.
Coordination and Facilitation of the CFT. The care coordinator shall:
Identify a location for the CFT meetings that is suitable to the participant’s needs;
Convene the CFT at the frequency described in Regulation .13A of this chapter; and
For 1915(i) participants, convene as per the timeline and functions pursuant to COMAR 10.09.89.
After an initial assessment, each participant shall be reassessed at a minimum of every 6 months.
Development and Periodic Revision of the POC.
After the initial assessment is completed, a POC shall be developed based on the information obtained through the comprehensive screening and assessment tools approved by the Department.
The CCO shall finalize the POC within 30 calendar days of notification of enrollment and submit it to the Department or its designee.
Development of and updates to the POC shall be youth- and family-directed and managed through CFT meetings.
The POC shall meet the requirements of Regulation .12 of this chapter.
The POC development process shall include:

The CFT meeting, which includes the participant, and if the participant is a minor, the minor’s parent or guardian, providers, family members, and other interested persons, as appropriate, for the purpose of establishing, revising, and reviewing the POC;
The development of the written, individualized POC based on the participant’s strengths, needs, and progress toward outcome measures;

Transitional care planning that involves contact with the participant or, if the participant is a minor, the minor’s parent or guardian, or the staff of a referring agency, or a service provider who is responsible to plan for continuity of care from inpatient level of care or an out-of-home placement to another type of community service; and

Discharge planning from care coordination, when appropriate and when the family is closer to its identified vision, when family needs have been met, and when outcome measures for care coordination have been achieved; and
After the POC is developed, the CCO shall update the POC as often as clinically indicated based on the strengths and needs of the participant but not less than:
For Level I participants, every 6 months;
For Level II participants, every 3 months;
For Level III participants, every 60 calendar days; and
For all participants, within 7 calendar days following a crisis event; and
For all participants, within 10 calendar days of every CFT meeting, to include changes in progress, services, or other areas.

section.heading: Covered Services — Child and Family Team Meetings.
section.text: The CCO shall:

para.text: Coordinate and facilitate the CFT, with CFT meetings convened as often as clinically necessary, but no less than:
Within 7 calendar days following a crisis event;
For Level I participants, every 6 months;
For Level II participants, every 3 months; and
For Level III participants, every 60 calendar days.

Record and keep notes at every CFT meeting that include the CFT members who were present, a summary of the discussion, any changes to the POC, and action items for follow up, and share them with the CFT members, including those who were not in attendance;
Update the POC in accordance with Regulation .11F(6) of this chapter; and
Ensure that the care coordinator:
Facilitates CFT meetings;
Facilitates access to the services and supports in the POC; and
At the first meeting:
Administers the appropriate assessments, as designated by the Department;
Works with the participant and family to develop an initial crisis plan that includes response to immediate service needs; and
Provides an overview of the care coordination process to all participants, including those receiving 1915(i) services.

para.text: The following terms have the meanings indicated.
Terms Defined.
“Advanced practice registered nurse (APRN)” means a registered nurse who is certified by the Board as:
A certified nurse midwife (CNM);
A certified registered nurse anesthetist (CRNA);
A certified nurse practitioner (CRNP); or
A clinical nurse specialist (CNS).
“Board” means the Maryland Board of Nursing.
“Certification” means the permission to practice as a PMH/APRN granted by the Board to a registered nurse who has:
Met all the specified requirements of a national certifying body recognized by the Board; and
Complied with the requirements of this chapter.
“Clinical nurse specialist” means an individual who:
Meets one of the following requirements:
Is licensed by the Board to practice registered nursing; or
Has a multistate licensure privilege to practice registered nursing under the Nurse Licensure Compact; and
Is certified by the Board to practice as a clinical nurse specialist.
“Durable medical equipment” means equipment that:
Can withstand repeated use;
Is used to serve a medically necessary purpose; and
Has no practical use in the absence of illness, injury, disability, or health condition.
“Mental health diagnosis” means the identification of a potential or an actual alteration in an individual's mental health status.
“Multistate licensure privilege” has the meaning set forth in COMAR 10.27.01.01B.
“Nurse psychotherapist” means a registered nurse, certified under this chapter, who utilizes psychotherapy in a formally structured, contractual relationship between the nurse therapist and a client.
Practice as a Clinical Nurse Specialist.
“Practice as a clinical nurse specialist” means to:
Provide direct care to patients with complex needs;
Act as a consultant to another health care provider as needed;
Conduct health-related research; and
Provide education and guidance for staff nurses.
“Practice as a clinical nurse specialist” includes:
Ordering, performing, and interpreting laboratory tests;
Ordering diagnostic tests and using the findings or results in the care of patients;
Prescribing drugs and durable medical equipment in accordance with Regulation .09 of this chapter;
Ordering home health and hospice care; and
Initiating, monitoring, and altering appropriate therapies or treatments.
“Psychiatric mental health/ advanced practice registered nurse (PMH/APRN)” means an advanced practice registered nurse who:
Is certified by the Board as a clinical nurse specialist in the practice of psychiatric mental health; and
Autonomously and independently engages in activities authorized by this chapter.

“Psychobiological interventions” means interventions which integrate physiological and psychological dimensions of care and include a range of therapies from diet/nutrition regulation, hypnosis, and relaxation techniques to the use of pharmacologic agents.
“Registered nurse (RN)” means an individual who:
Is licensed by the Board to practice registered nursing; or
Has a multistate licensure privilege to practice registered nursing.

section.heading: Scope of Practice.
section.text: The PMH/APRN may perform independently, autonomously, and in a variety of settings, the following functions:

para.text: Establish a mental health diagnosis;
Evaluate and manage psychobiological intervention;
Provide individual therapy;
Provide group therapy;
Provide family therapy;
Provide any therapy which purpose is to:
Effect change in the client in an attempt to alleviate emotional disturbances,
Reverse or change maladaptive behavior, and
Facilitate personality growth and development;
Provide consultation to health care providers and others to:
Influence the plans of care for clients,
Enhance the abilities of others to provide behavioral, psychiatric, and mental health care, and
Effect change in systems;
Provide behavioral rehabilitation care;
Utilize advanced practice skills to independently provide:
Case management including psychiatric rehabilitation and home care; and
Health teaching, health promotion, health prevention, and aid to the client in developing healthy patterns of living; and
Prescribe drugs and durable medical equipment in accordance with Regulation .09 of this chapter.
Advanced practice skills shall be utilized in the collection of relevant data to comprehensively assess the client's mental health.
Assessment data shall be analyzed in determining diagnosis.
A plan of care shall be developed and implemented that outlines treatment to attain expected outcomes.

Individual, group, and family psychotherapy, child psychotherapy, behavioral care, case management, and other therapeutic treatments shall be utilized to assist clients in fostering mental health, preventing mental illness and disability, and improving or regaining previous health status and functional abilities.
Advanced knowledge of psychobiological interventions shall be utilized and clinical skills applied to maximize the client's health and prevent further disability.
The client's progress in attaining the expected outcome shall be evaluated.
Strategies and interventions shall be utilized to promote and maintain health and prevent mental illness.
Decisions and actions on behalf of clients shall be determined in an ethical manner.
Medical records shall be maintained in accordance with Health-General Article, Title 4, Subtitle 4, Annotated Code of Maryland.
A clinical nurse specialist may prescribe any drug or durable medical equipment within the clinical nurse specialist’s education, training, certification, and population foci.
A clinical nurse specialist when exercising prescriptive authority is accountable for competency in:
Problem identification through appropriate assessment;
Medication and device selection;
Patient education for use of therapeutics;
Knowledge of interactions of therapeutics;
Evaluation of outcome; and
Recognition and management of side effects, adverse reactions, and complications.

para.text: In this chapter, the following terms have the meanings indicated.
Terms Defined.
“Advanced practice registered nurse (APRN)” means a registered nurse who is certified by the Board as:
A certified nurse midwife (CNM);
A certified registered nurse anesthetist (CRNA);
A certified nurse practitioner (CRNP); or
A clinical nurse specialist (CNS).
“Board” means the Board of Nursing.
“Certification” means the permission to practice as a clinical nurse specialist granted by the Board to a registered nurse who:
Met all the specified requirements of a national certifying body recognized by the Board; and
Complied with the requirements of this chapter.
“Clinical nurse specialist” means an individual who:
Meets one of the following requirements:
Is licensed by the Board to practice registered nursing; or
Has a multistate licensure privilege to practice registered nursing under the Nurse Licensure Compact; and
Is certified by the Board to practice as a clinical nurse specialist.
“Durable medical equipment” means equipment that:
Can withstand repeated use;
Is used to serve a medically necessary purpose; and
Has no practical use in the absence of illness, injury, disability, or health condition.
“Multistate licensure privilege” has the meaning set forth in COMAR 10.27.01.01B.
Practice as a Clinical Nurse Specialist.
“Practice as a clinical nurse specialist” means to:
Provide direct care to patients with complex needs;
Act as a consultant to another health care provider as needed;
Conduct health-related research; and
Provide education and guidance for staff nurses.
“Practice as a clinical nurse specialist” includes:
Ordering, performing, and interpreting laboratory tests;
Ordering diagnostic tests and using the findings or results in the care of patients;
Prescribing drugs and durable medical equipment in accordance with Regulation .07 of this chapter;
Ordering home health and hospice care; and
Initiating, monitoring, and altering appropriate therapies or treatments.
“Registered nurse (RN)” means an individual who:
Is licensed by the Board to practice registered nursing; or
Has a multistate licensure privilege to practice registered nursing.

“Standard of care” means the practice of clinical nurse specialist in accordance with the core competencies and behaviors specified by the National Association of Clinical Nurse Specialists (NACNS), American Nurse Credentialing Center (ANCC), American Association of Critical Care Nurses (AACN), or other national certifying body recognized by the Board.
“System” means the setting within which the clinical nurse specialist is practicing.
The unique role of the clinical nurse specialist is to:
Integrate care across the continuum and through three spheres of impact:
The patient;
The nurse; and
The system; and
Ranges from:
Wellness to illness; and
Acute to chronic care.
The clinical nurse specialist:
Provides direct care to patients with complex needs;
Acts as a consultant;
Leads evidence-based practice;
Conducts research;
Optimizes organizational systems;
Advances nursing practice; and
Provides education and guidance for staff nurses.
The practice of the clinical nurse specialist encompasses the continuous improvement of patient outcomes and nursing care.
The clinical nurse specialist shall adhere to standards of clinical practice, scope of practice, and the standards of professional performance in accordance with this chapter.
The scope of practice of the clinical nurse specialist includes, but is not limited to:
Practicing with individual clients, families, groups, and populations of clients;
Performing a comprehensive physical assessment of patients;
Establishing medical diagnosis for short-term and chronic stable health problems;
Diagnosing, treating, and managing patients with acute and chronic illness or disease, or both;
Ordering, performing, and interpreting laboratory tests;
Ordering diagnostic tests and using the findings or results in the care of patients
Initiating, monitoring, and altering appropriate therapies or treatments;
Providing primary health care, including health promotion and disease prevention by assessing, educating, and providing referrals for the patient, family, and caregiver;
Providing emergency care;
Referring patients to appropriate licensed physicians or other health care providers as needed;
Consulting or collaborating with a licensed physician or other health care provider as needed;
Creating therapeutic environments through mentoring and system changes to:
Develop caring, evidence-based practices;
Facilitate ethical decision making;
Respond to diversity; and
Alleviate patient distress; and
Prescribing drugs and durable medical equipment in accordance with Regulation .07 of this chapter.
The clinical nurse specialist shall maintain medical records in accordance with Health-General Article, Title 4, Subtitle 4, Annotated Code of Maryland.
Unless certified as a clinical nurse specialist under this chapter, an individual may not:
Practice as a clinical nurse specialist;
Represent to the public by title, description of services, method, procedure, or otherwise that the individual is authorized to practice as a clinical nurse specialist; or
Use the following words or abbreviations:
Certified clinical nurse specialist;
Clinical nurse specialist (CNS);
Clinical Registered Nurse Specialist (CRNS);
Clinical nurse specialist graduate; or
Nurse specialist graduate.

An individual certified under this chapter shall be subject to disciplinary prohibitions and sanctions set forth under Health Occupations Article, §§8-316 and 8-701 — 8-703, Annotated Code of Maryland.
A clinical nurse specialist may prescribe any drug or durable medical equipment within the clinical nurse specialist’s education, training, certification, and population foci.
A clinical nurse specialist, when exercising prescriptive authority, is accountable for competency in:
Problem identification through appropriate assessment;
Medication and device selection;
Patient education for use of therapeutics;
Knowledge of interactions of therapeutics;
Evaluation of outcome; and
Recognition and management of side effects, adverse reactions, and complications.
